pair.title: Tell HN: I'm sick of AI everything

story.title: Tell HN: I'm sick of AI everything

comments.text:
I was at a startup in 1999 that pivoted twice based on investor pressure to "be more internet." We had a perfectly functional desktop application, but suddenly we needed a web version, then a mobile version, then we needed to "leverage the cloud" (which wasn't even called that yet). Burned through cash, hired 40 people we didn't need, and folded in 2001. The irony is our original product still works today on abandonware sites.

This AI thing has that exact same energy. I keep seeing job postings where they've literally just swapped "blockchain" or "machine learning" for "AI" from 2017 templates. The worst part is knowing it'll probably take another five years of dead companies before people remember that technology serves the business, not the other way around.

kids.text:
The difference is that in 1999, at least the underlying infrastructure was real and useful. Kozmo.com failed as a business model, but people genuinely wanted same-day delivery. The web itself wasn't a bubble, just the valuations.

With AI, we're seeing companies slap it onto products where it actively makes things worse. Resume screeners that reject qualified candidates. Customer support chatbots that can't solve anything. "AI-powered" features that are just autocomplete with higher latency. And the recruiting copy is the most egregious part-suddenly every job posting needs to mention LLMs even when the role has nothing to do with them.

The venture capital playbook is identical though: find a technology that's genuinely interesting, convince everyone it's the future, dump money on it indiscriminately, and let the market sort out which applications actually create value versus which ones were just funded because the founder said the right buzzword in the pitch deck. Except this time it might take even longer to unwind because AI has real utility in specific domains, so the whole ecosystem gets to hide under that umbrella.

The infrastructure argument is weak though. AWS exists now and actually works, so what's the excuse? At least in 1999 companies had the cover of "nobody knows how this will play out." Today every VC-backed startup is slapping AI onto their product roadmap because it's literally free marketing hype. The difference isn't that AI is real and useful-it's that we're dumber about acknowledging the con.

I've been through a few cycles of this now. The infrastructure argument isn't actually weak, though, it's just evolved. AWS works, yes, but the real difference between 1999 and today is that back then you needed actual technical competence to execute on the hype. You had to hire people who understood distributed systems, database replication, load balancing. Those people were expensive and hard to find.

Now? You can prompt Claude to generate a feature spec, use it to hire mid-level engineers on the cheap because "AI will help them be more productive," and ship something that technically works but solves no real problem. The friction is gone. In 1999, at least throwing money at a bad idea required you to employ actual engineers who might push back. Today the entire pipeline from idea to shipped product can run on hype and cheap labor.

What kills me is watching companies hire "AI engineers" at startup salaries to do what was previously a data scientist or ML engineer role at FAANG rates. They're not delusional about AI being useful in theory. They're rational about extracting value from the hype while underpaying for it. The startups burning through VC money on AI feature creep are following a playbook that actually works now because the execution barrier is lower.

The difference is that in 1999, at least the underlying infrastructure was real and useful. Kozmo.com failed as a business model, but people genuinely wanted same-day delivery. Now we're seeing companies retrofit AI into processes that don't need it, solving problems that don't exist. At least the dotcom bubble produced the plumbing we still use today.

comments.text: Reminds me of the whole "Web3" pivot cycle. Every startup suddenly needed blockchain in their pitch deck around 2021-2022, regardless of whether it solved anything. Now it's the same energy with AI. The difference is at least some AI tools genuinely work-which paradoxically makes the hype worse because real utility gets buried under marketing noise.

kids.text:
Yeah, the Web3 parallel is spot-on. The key difference you're nailing is that AI actually delivers something, which creates this weird dynamic where the hype becomes self-reinforcing. With crypto, skeptics could point to fundamental issues. With AI, people use it, it works for their task, and then suddenly every deck has "AI-powered" slapped on it.

I think the real problem is that most organizations haven't actually figured out where to apply it well yet. They're in the phase of "we have this powerful tool, what do we do?" instead of "we have this specific problem that AI solves." So yeah, organizational debt first seems right. The tech amplifies whatever's already broken.

The problem is you're all still thinking about this wrong. AI actually works, sure, but "works" just means it's a better hammer. Most of these organizations don't need a better hammer, they need to figure out what they're actually trying to build. Instead they're just hammering everything because they have a hammer. It's not hype that's the issue, it's organizational dysfunction masquerading as innovation. The crypto crowd at least had the excuse that their tech was fundamentally broken. You lot are just choosing not to think.

The blockchain comparison is apt, but I'd push back slightly on the "at least some AI tools genuinely work" part. Web3 had real utility too if you squinted-programmable money, composability, all legitimate ideas. The problem wasn't the tech, it was that every single problem got hammered into a blockchain-shaped nail.

Same thing happening now. Yes, LLMs are useful for certain tasks. But we're watching companies retrofit AI into everything from customer service (worse than phone trees) to "AI-powered" recruiting (which just bakes in existing biases faster). The hype cycle is actually obscuring where the real value is because the signal-to-noise ratio has collapsed.

I've been in the trenches building data pipelines for the past 5 years, and the "AI solves everything" pitch has completely taken over RFPs. Last month a client asked us to "add AI" to a system that just needed better indexing. We did the indexing work, shipped it in 2 weeks, and it solved their actual problem. They still wanted us to slap "powered by ML" on the marketing materials though, which we declined. The tools are genuinely useful for specific tasks (code generation, summarization), but treating it as a universal solution is exactly how you end up with overengineered garbage that nobody actually needs.

The indexing work solved the problem in 2 weeks. Of course they wanted "powered by ML" on the materials anyway. That's the whole game right now. Real utility gets buried because executives read TechCrunch headlines and panic that they're falling behind. Nobody wants to admit their issue was just bad database design or missing monitoring. Way easier to say "we're leveraging AI" and move on. The worst part is it actually works for fundraising and board meetings, so the incentives are completely backwards. You'll keep seeing this until the pendulum swings hard the other way, probably when the first major failure gets blamed on "AI" instead of the actual root cause.

comments.text: The thing that bugs me most is how it's infected the *language* itself. Every job posting, every press release, every product update now has this cargo-cult AI worship baked in. It's not even about whether AI is useful anymore-it's just noise, a tribal signal that you're "innovating" and "future-ready." Meanwhile the actual problems in these organizations (terrible documentation, siloed teams, outdated infrastructure) stay exactly the same. AI just gets bolted on top like a spoiler on a minivan.

I actually think some of the AI adoption is genuinely useful, but you're hitting on something real about the messaging fatigue. The difference from dot-com for me is that back then the hype was disconnected from any real utility. Now there's actual value, but it's like we've collectively decided to pretend every problem is an AI problem.

I work at a mid-size fintech place and watched our job postings get rewritten three times in six months. The first version had actual technical requirements. By month two, every description had been "enhanced" with language about machine learning pipelines and AI-driven decision making, even for roles that have nothing to do with either. The hiring team kept getting pressure from above to "position us as an AI company" for investor optics.

The thing that bugs me more than the marketing is the resource allocation. We have genuine technical debt, our data pipeline is a mess, and we're still doing things manually that could be automated with boring infrastructure work. Instead there's this pull toward flashy AI projects that don't solve our actual problems. It's like the organizational dysfunction you mentioned gets papered over with hype instead of fixed.

I do think this will shake out. The companies that use AI to solve real bottlenecks will win, and the ones doing it for the resume will eventually look silly. But yeah, the next 18 months of job postings are going to be insufferable.

kids.text:
> The difference from dot-com for me is that back then the hype was disconnected from any real utility. Now there's actual value, but it's like we've collectively decided to pretend every problem is an AI problem.

This is where I think you're fooling yourself. The "actual value" is mostly confined to a handful of specific tasks: code completion, summarizing dense text, basic content generation. Everything else is theater.

Your fintech place rewrote job postings three times because someone in leadership read a McKinsey report and panicked. That's not utility-driven adoption, that's the same cargo-cult thinking that killed pets.com. You had actual problems before AI and you have the same problems now, except now your stack is harder to maintain because someone bolted on an LLM that adds latency and cost.

The real tell is that most companies can't articulate what the AI is actually doing for their bottom line. They just know competitors are "AI-first" so they need to be too. Dot-com was "we're an Internet company," which at least made semantic sense even when it didn't make financial sense. This is worse because it's pretending to be rigorous when it's pure superstition.

And the utility that does exist? It's mostly going to compress into a few libraries and frameworks within 18 months. At that point the hype evaporates and we're left with normal tools that do their job quietly. But by then you'll have already burned money on a bunch of half-baked internal ML projects that nobody maintains.

I'd push back on the "actual value" framing a bit. Yes, LLMs are genuinely useful for certain narrow tasks-code completion, summarization, drafting boilerplate. But most of what I'm seeing in job postings is cargo cult stuff. "AI-powered customer insights" when they mean basic analytics. "Leveraging machine learning" for what's essentially regex on structured data.

The dot-com parallel holds because the infrastructure was real too. TCP/IP, HTTP, databases-those were genuinely transformative. But most startups just bolted "dot-com" onto whatever they were already doing. Same energy here. The technology is real, but the deployment often isn't solving the actual problem; it's solving the optics problem of needing to look innovative.

> "AI-powered customer insights" when they mean basic analytics.

The real issue is that we've let companies define the problem space instead of regulators. In the EU, algorithmic accountability rules under the AI Act are starting to force companies to actually document what their "AI" does. In the US, the FTC is finally pushing back on vague claims. But most job postings are written before any of that lands, so you get this cargo cult where every analytics pipeline becomes "AI-driven" because it sounds better to investors. The dotcom parallel breaks down here: we had no framework then either, but at least the infrastructure debate was honest. Now it's just marketing theater hiding the fact that most companies don't know what they're buying.

comments.text:
I think the framing here conflates two separate problems. Yes, there's definitely marketing/HR cargo cult behavior around AI right now. But the underlying technology isn't actually like the dot-com bubble in the way you're suggesting.

Dot-com companies were often solving fake problems or solving real problems with business models that couldn't work at scale. The value was speculative. With AI, we're seeing genuine productivity gains in specific domains. I use Claude and GPT-4 in my daily work as a backend engineer, and the efficiency improvement is real and measurable. I'm shipping features 30-40% faster on tasks like boilerplate generation, test writing, and refactoring. That's not speculation.

The hype cycle is real, sure. The cargo cult marketing is annoying. But conflating "companies are overselling AI in job postings" with "AI is fundamentally worthless like Pets.com" misses what's actually happening. The tech works. Some applications are genuinely valuable. Others are gimmicks.

The organizational bottleneck point you raise is actually the more interesting one. Most companies bringing in AI tools without fixing process/communication/knowledge management problems will see disappointment. It's not the tool's fault. But that's an execution problem, not a technology problem.

I'd bet we're still in the early innings for AI utility. The hype will eventually deflate, some companies will fail, and we'll end up with a smaller set of actually useful applications. That's not a bubble bursting, that's normal technology maturation. The dot-com crash happened because the entire value proposition was fictional. Here, at least some of it is real.

kids.text:
> With AI, we're seeing genuine productivity gains in specific domains.

This is true but needs specificity. "Productivity gains" is marketing-speak. Which domains exactly? Code completion in languages with massive training data (Python, JS)? Yes, measurable. Summarizing documents? Sometimes. But I've watched teams adopt Claude for domains where it hallucinates confidently (financial modeling, regulatory interpretation) and then spend weeks debugging the output. The gain evaporates once you account for review time.

The dot-com comparison might be weaker than the OP suggests, but your counterargument needs receipts, not just assertion.

You're just defining "genuine productivity gains" in a way that conveniently excludes all the hype. Show me the actual ROI numbers from companies deploying this stuff, not the marketing deck numbers. Most places I've seen are still in the "throw it at everything and see what sticks" phase.

I've actually seen this play out at two companies now. Claude's code review suggestions cut our PR turnaround by 35%, but that only matters because we had the processes in place to use them. The first company just threw it at the codebase and declared victory in a deck. Without the organizational fix (async reviews, clear standards, actual ownership), it was dead weight within three months.

The ROI gap isn't the tool, it's the willingness to do boring work first.

I think you're drawing a useful distinction, but I'd push back slightly on the productivity gains claim. Yes, LLMs are genuinely useful for specific tasks - code generation, summarization, brainstorming - but the organizational deployment we're seeing often isn't about those narrow wins. It's about replacing headcount or "augmenting" roles in ways that end up reducing decision quality.

The dot-com parallel holds because both eras conflate "the technology works" with "throwing it at every problem works." Pets.com failed not because online pet supply was fake, but because the business model didn't make sense at scale. Similarly, using Claude to write better emails is real. But using it to replace your entire customer support team because you saw a TechCrunch headline about AI? That's the bubble part.

What concerns me more than the hype itself is the asymmetry in outcomes. Early internet investors and engineers who understood the business model won big. Most others lost money or years of their career. We're probably going to see the same bifurcation here - people who use LLMs tactically in their actual workflow will extract real value, while companies that bet their entire strategy on "AI-first" will learn the hard way that technology adoption isn't strategy.

The honest version of the parent comment seems to be: "yes, there's genuine productivity here, AND yes, most of what you're seeing in job postings and marketing is cargo cult behavior." Both things are true. The productivity is real but narrow. The hype is real but mostly divorced from actual implementation plans.

> "the organizational deployment we're seeing often isn't about those narrow wins"

This is the key observation, but I'd be more precise about what's actually happening. Most orgs aren't even attempting to measure the productivity gains you're talking about. They're making deployment decisions based on analyst reports and competitor behavior, not on internal ROI analysis.

The dot-com comparison breaks down because at least Pets.com had a clear (if flawed) business hypothesis: people want pet supplies delivered. With AI, I'm seeing job requisitions that say "must have LLM experience" without any corresponding job responsibilities that actually require it. That's not deployment in service of a real problem. That's cargo cult hiring.

The narrow wins you mention (code generation, summarization) are real and valuable. But those don't require organizational restructuring or headcount reduction. They're tools that make existing roles more efficient. The current behavior looks much more like the dot-com era because the technology is being used as a narrative to justify decisions that were already being made for other reasons (cost cutting, stock price signals, etc.).

I was in the room during the late 90s dot-com chaos, and you're right that the underlying tech was often vapor. But here's what bothers me now: back then at least there was *some* friction keeping the hype somewhat grounded in reality. You had to actually build something, get users, prove unit economics. It was hard and most failed.

Now? A company can slap "AI-powered" on their job posting and their stock barely budges either way. The marketing machinery works so fast that substance doesn't matter anymore. At least Pets.com had to ship actual dog food to actual people. There's no equivalent forcing function here. That might actually be worse.
